title: Divide Decimals

projects.title: Cooking Simulator — Decimal Operations
projects.desc: Divide decimals to portion recipes in this cooking simulator (6.NS.3).

projects.title: Decimal Division Drive (Part 1)
projects.desc: Interactive graphic novel: divide decimals to drive the mission forward (6.NS.3).

contentObjective: I can divide with decimals by making the divisor a whole number first.
languageObjective: I can explain my work using the words dividend, divisor, quotient, and equivalent division.

noticeAndWonder.context: A school bought new math workbooks as shown in the order form. What is the cost of each workbook? How can you determine the cost of each workbook?
wonderStarters: I wonder what would happen if…

revealWordProblem.title: Apply: Running a Marathon
revealWordProblem.text: Evelyn is training to run a marathon on the path shown. Evelyn expects that she will need at least 4 hours to finish the race but hopes to finish in less than 5 hours. How many miles should Evelyn try to run each hour to achieve her goal?

turnAndTalk.question: The station has 18.9 kilograms of mineral to divide into 6.3-kilogram pods. Why can't you divide by the decimal 6.3 directly the same way you divide by a whole number?
turnAndTalk.kernel: You first make the divisor a whole number using equivalent division.

stems.en: I make the divisor 6.3 into a ___ before dividing.
stems.es: Convierto el divisor 6.3 en un ___ antes de dividir.

stems.en: Dividing by a decimal directly is hard because ___.
stems.es: Dividir entre un decimal directamente es difícil porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain you move the decimal in the divisor to make it whole (6.3 to 63) and move the dividend the same way, naming 18.9 as the dividend and 6.3 as the divisor.
turnAndTalk.extend: Estimate how many pods can be filled before computing, then justify. About how many 6-kg groups fit in 18.9 kg?
extendStems: I estimate about ___ pods because 18.9 is close to ___.

turnAndTalk.question: When you divided 18.9 by 6.3, how did you use equivalent division to make the divisor a whole number?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Move both decimal points the same number of places so the value stays equal.

stems.en: I moved both decimal points ___ place, changing it to ___ ÷ ___.
stems.es: Moví ambos puntos decimales ___ lugar, cambiándolo a ___ ÷ ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students rewrite 18.9 ÷ 6.3 as 189 ÷ 63 = 3, explaining moving both points one place keeps the division equivalent, so 3 pods are filled.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why does moving BOTH decimal points the same number of places keep the answer the same? Justify with what you know about equivalent fractions.
extendStems: Moving both points keeps the answer the same because ___.
It is like multiplying the top and bottom of a fraction by ___, which ___.

turnAndTalk.question: A ribbon is 16.8 feet long and you cut pieces that are each 2.4 feet. How does decimal division tell you how many pieces you can cut?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Use equivalent division to make 2.4 whole, then divide.

stems.en: I can cut ___ pieces because I divided 16.8 by ___.
stems.es: Puedo cortar ___ piezas porque dividí 16.8 entre ___.

stems.en: I moved both decimals to change it to ___ ÷ ___.
stems.es: Moví ambos decimales para cambiarlo a ___ ÷ ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students rewrite 16.8 ÷ 2.4 as 168 ÷ 24 = 7, so 7 pieces can be cut with none left over.
turnAndTalk.extend: How can you check that 7 pieces is correct without redividing? Show the check.
extendStems: If the product equals the ribbon length, then ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Why does making the divisor a whole number first make dividing by a decimal so much easier?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Equivalent division turns a decimal divisor into a familiar whole-number problem.

stems.en: Making the divisor a whole number helps because ___.
stems.es: Convertir el divisor en un número entero ayuda porque ___.

stems.en: After moving the points, the problem becomes a ___ division.
stems.es: Después de mover los puntos, el problema se vuelve una división de ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain that a whole-number divisor lets them divide normally, and equivalent division keeps the quotient the same while removing the decimal from the divisor.
turnAndTalk.extend: In 19.2 ÷ 0.8, how many places do you move each decimal point, and why does the answer get larger than 19.2? Explain.
extendStems: I move each point ___ place, making it ___ ÷ ___.
The quotient is larger than 19.2 because dividing by less than 1 ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Divide Decimals, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.

stems.en: I moved both decimal points ___ place, changing it to ___ ÷ ___.
stems.es: Moví ambos puntos decimales ___ lugar, cambiándolo a ___ ÷ ___.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.NS.3 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

vocabulary.definition: The number you are splitting up in a division problem.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número que estás repartiendo en una división.
vocabulary.visual: In 8.4 ÷ 2.1 = 4, the dividend is 8.4 — it is the total being split
vocabulary.cloze: In a division problem, the number being divided is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Số bị chia trong phép chia.
vocabulary.definitionAr: الرقم الذي يتم تقسيمه في عملية القسمة.

vocabulary.definition: The number you split by in a division problem.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número entre el que divides en una división.
vocabulary.visual: In 8.4 ÷ 2.1 = 4, the divisor is 2.1 — it is the size of each group
vocabulary.cloze: In a division problem, the number we divide by is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Số dùng để chia cho số khác trong phép chia.

vocabulary.visual: In 8.4 ÷ 2.1 = 4, the quotient is 4 — there are 4 groups
vocabulary.cloze: The answer to a division problem is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionAr: النتيجة التي نحصل عليها من عملية القسمة.

vocabulary.definition: Dividing with decimals. First make the number you divide by a whole number.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Dividir con decimales. Primero haz entero el número entre el que divides.
vocabulary.cloze: Dividing numbers that include a decimal point is called ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Phép toán chia một số thập phân cho một số khác.
vocabulary.definitionAr: عملية قسمة عدد عشري على رقم آخر.

vocabulary.definition: Multiplying both numbers by 10 or 100 gives the same answer.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Multiplicar ambos números por 10 o 100 da la misma respuesta.
vocabulary.cloze: Multiplying both the dividend and divisor by the same power of 10 creates an ___ that is easier to solve.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Các biểu thức chia khác nhau nhưng có cùng một thương số.
vocabulary.definitionAr: عمليات قسمة مختلفة تعطي نفس خارج القسمة.

launch.narrative: The space station has 18.9 kilograms of a rare mineral sample that must be divided equally among 6.3-kilogram storage pods. How many pods can be completely filled?
launch.contextImage: Space station science lab with glowing mineral samples being weighed on a digital scale next to storage pods
noticePrompts: What total amount is being divided?
What is the size of each pod?
wonderPrompts: How do you divide when the divisor is a decimal?
Can you turn this into a whole number division problem?

conceptIntro.heading: How do you divide with decimals?
conceptIntro.intro: Dividing splits a total into equal groups. When you divide by a decimal, you first make the divisor (the number you divide by) a whole number, so the division is easier.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: I want 18.9 ÷ 6.3. The divisor is 6.3, and I want it to be a whole number.
I move the decimal point 1 place right in the divisor: 6.3 becomes 63.
I must move the dividend the same 1 place right: 18.9 becomes 189. Now the problem is 189 ÷ 63.
I check by multiplying: 6.3 × 3 = 18.9. Correct — 3 pods can be filled.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Let's do 8.4 ÷ 2.1. How many places do we move to make 2.1 a whole number? (1 place, so 2.1 becomes 21.)
Move the dividend the same way: 8.4 becomes 84. What is the new problem? (84 ÷ 21.)

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next, you will rewrite decimal divisions as whole-number divisions and solve them on your own.
Remember: move the point the same number of places in both numbers.

explore.instructions: Complete the table. Rewrite each decimal division as a whole-number division by multiplying both the dividend and divisor by 10 (or 100), then solve.

discourse.prompt: Why does multiplying both the dividend and divisor by the same power of 10 give you the same quotient?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Multiplying both numbers by ___ doesn't change the quotient because ___.

optionalActivity.name: Decimal Cafe Portion Lab
optionalActivity.intro: Welcome to the Decimal Cafe — divide money, recipes, and game tokens into equal portions to keep every table served.

optional.stem: A ribbon is 7.2 meters long and is cut into 0.8-meter pieces. How many pieces are there?

optional.explanation: Dividing by 0.25 is like multiplying by 4: 15 × 4 = 60.

optional.stem: Table 1 splits a $15.00 lunch bill equally among 4 friends. How much does each friend pay?
A recipe uses 9.6 cups of flour to make 3 equal batches of muffins. How many cups of flour go in each batch?
The arcade corner has 4.8 meters of token ribbon, cut into 0.6-meter prize strips. How many strips can the cafe make?

optional.label: Match each cafe order to its quotient to unlock the table number.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — The Misplaced Decimal at the Register

workedExample.label: Order
workedExample.work: A $13.50 catering tray is split into 0.45-pound scoops. How many scoops?

optional.correctWork: Moving both points 2 places gives 1350 ÷ 45, which equals 30, not 3.0 — the decimal point was placed one spot too far left. So 13.50 ÷ 0.45 = 30 scoops. Check: 30 × 0.45 = 13.50. ✓
hints: After you rewrite it as 1350 ÷ 45, estimate first: about how many 45s fit in 1350? That is much more than 3.
1350 ÷ 45 = 30. The cashier slid the decimal one place too far left, turning 30 into 3.0.

approaching.label: Rewrite each problem by making the divisor a whole number, then solve.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Divide Decimals.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.

onLevel.label: Match each decimal division to its quotient.

onLevel.stem: A ribbon is 16.8 feet long. You cut it into pieces that are each 2.4 feet long. How many pieces do you get?

onLevel.instructions: Sort: should you multiply both numbers by 10 or by 100 to make the divisor a whole number?

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Divide Decimals.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same.

extending.title: Find Derek's Mistake
extending.correctWork: Derek only multiplied by 10, but the divisor 0.45 has two decimal places. He should multiply both numbers by 100: 720 ÷ 45 = 16. He got the right answer by luck this time, but his method is unreliable because the divisor is still a decimal (4.5).
hints: How many decimal places does 0.45 have? Did Derek multiply by enough to make it a whole number?
0.45 has two decimal places, so multiply both by 100, not 10. 0.45 × 100 = 45 and 7.2 × 100 = 720.

extending.prompt: A gas station sells fuel at $3.45 per gallon. A customer pays $27.60 total. How many gallons did the customer buy? Set up the decimal division, show how to convert it to whole-number division, and solve.
extending.sentenceFrame: The equation is $27.60 ÷ $3.45 = ___. I multiply both by ___ to get ___ ÷ ___ = ___ gallons.

extending.instructions: Verify each division by showing that quotient × divisor = dividend. Are the two sides balanced?

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Divide Decimals is skipping the key idea: "Move the decimal point the same number of places in BOTH the divisor and the dividend; the answer stays the same." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: A ribbon is 16.8 feet long. You need to cut it into pieces that are each 2.4 feet long for a craft project.
connect.promptQuestion: How does decimal division help you figure out how many pieces you can cut?
